title: River Dolphins in India
focus: Population status, species differentiation (Gangetic vs. Indus), biological characteristics, and conservation initiatives.
rationale: This section provides a comprehensive overview of India's river dolphins, making it a distinct and highly examinable topic for UPSC, especially regarding species comparison, IUCN status, and specific conservation zones.
sectionExcerpt: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change and Wildlife Institute of India (WII) released 'Population Status of River Dolphins in India 2024'... India’s River dolphin population: 6327- Gangetic River dolphins (6324), Indus River Dolphins (only 3)... Ganges and Indus River Dolphins are two distinct species based on genetic and morphological data. Functionally blind, and rely on echolocation to navigate and hunt... Gangetic Dolphin Specifics: National Aquatic Animal of India... Indus River Dolphin Specifics: Punjab’s State Aquatic Animal... Conservation Measures: Project Dolphin (2020), Vikramshila Gangetic Dolphin Wildlife Sanctuary, Bihar, India’s first National Dolphin Research Centre (NDRC) at Patna.
evidenceBullets: India's river dolphin population is 6327, comprising 6324 Gangetic and only 3 Indus River Dolphins.
Both Gangetic and Indus River Dolphins are listed as Endangered (EN) on the IUCN Red List and Schedule I of WPA, 1972.
River dolphins are functionally blind and rely on echolocation to navigate and hunt.
The Gangetic Dolphin is the National Aquatic Animal of India, while the Indus River Dolphin is Punjab's State Aquatic Animal.
Key conservation initiatives include Project Dolphin, Vikramshila Gangetic Dolphin Wildlife Sanctuary in Bihar, and India's first National Dolphin Research Centre (NDRC) at Patna.

title: Vultures in India and Conservation Efforts
focus: WII assessment findings, IUCN statuses of the 9 vulture species, threats from NSAIDs, and conservation initiatives.
rationale: Vultures are a classic UPSC environment topic. The detailed breakdown of 9 species and their IUCN statuses, along with specific conservation sites and threats, forms a distinct and critical study block.
sectionExcerpt: Wildlife Institute of India (WII) releases its 1st Pan-India Assessment and Monitoring of Endangered Species (Vultures)... Focus: Four Critically Endangered species i.e., the White-rumped, Indian, Slender-billed, and Red-headed vultures at 216 sites across 17 states... Threats: Ingestion of toxic non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) like diclofenac (used to treat livestock)... Conservation Initiatives: Vulture Conservation Breeding Centre (VCBC)- at Rani (Assam), Pinjore (Haryana)... White-rumped Vulture (Gyps bengalensis): Critically Endangered (CR)... Egyptian Vulture (Neophron percnopterus): Endangered (EN).
evidenceBullets: Four vulture species in India are Critically Endangered (CR): White-rumped, Indian, Slender-billed, and Red-headed vultures.
The Egyptian Vulture is Endangered (EN), while Bearded, Cinereous, and Himalayan Griffon are Near Threatened (NT).
A major threat to vultures is the ingestion of toxic non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) like diclofenac used to treat livestock.
Vulture Conservation Breeding Centres (VCBC) are located at Rani (Assam) and Pinjore (Haryana).
Protected Areas hold 54% of all documented vulture nests according to the WII assessment.

title: Draft Policy Paper on Camels and Camel Conservation
focus: Population decline drivers, habitat, pastoralist communities, and biological adaptations of camels.
rationale: The draft policy paper introduces a new focus on camel conservation, highlighting specific pastoralist communities and unique biological traits that are highly relevant for UPSC prelims.
sectionExcerpt: Draft policy paper on Camels, prepared by Ministry of Fisheries, Animal Husbandry and Dairying and Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) has proposed National Camel Sustainability Initiative (NCSI)... India’s camel population declined by more than 75% since the 1970s... Habitat: Dryland ecosystems (referred as “Ship of the Desert”) and are primarily reared (90%) in Rajasthan and Gujarat. Camel-rearing Pastoralist communities: Raika, Rabari, Fakirani Jat, and Manganiyar... Camels' humps store fats providing them energy when food is scarce and they store water in their blood cells, not their humps.
evidenceBullets: India's camel population has declined by over 75% since the 1970s.
90% of camels in India are reared in the dryland ecosystems of Rajasthan and Gujarat.
Key camel-rearing pastoralist communities include the Raika, Rabari, Fakirani Jat, and Manganiyar.
Camels store fat in their humps for energy, while water is stored in their blood cells, not their humps.
The National Camel Sustainability Initiative (NCSI) was proposed by the Ministry of Fisheries, Animal Husbandry and Dairying and the FAO.
